speaker: interviewer
text: Doing Mr. Godel Silber. And this is tape number 3. OK, Mr. Silber, tell us, where did the gun powder come from? Who-- how did it get smuggled from-- tell us the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the camp. There was-- there was-- was  a factory, the union-- union.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: An ammunition factory-- the union. So the girls worked over there. So-- and I had my girlfriend from Ciecanhow. And we were talking. And she-- she-- the girl's brother-- that, I told you. And-- that's right. And she gave it to me. And if I was in nighttime, if she went, she didn't give it to me. She give it to my wife. And she gave it that girl from Degania in Israel. But she had a stroke, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so they brought the ammunition-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon me. The gunpowder, OK, to you. OK, And you created what? How-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have created only hand grenade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hand grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that? You--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- OK. See I-- like, I was mechanic. So I had a little shack. I would say, I have-- I have that room. I have the-- I have the-- the-- this-- this room. Had over there welding. See, my job in-- in-- in Birkenau was to hang lamps and shades. You have a-- you ever been in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Anybody was in Birkenau? To hang the lead-- to make the shades. You know where the crematorium is-- the-- the poles. It was our-- the pole-- the poles from-- from the-- from Birkenau? The cement poles, like you hang the shades?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I use my hand? So the shade is here. Here is the shade. That's a shade. In here, goes the socket. This lit around all the camps with the number A, B, C, like that. They were lit around the camps. So I made the shades. And here had to be a piece of pipe, like this, like a handle to hold on. So there was the cement poles. In the cement pole was two-- two-- two holes. And see, I weld up two brackets. I had to weld up two brackets-- one on the top, and one on the bottom. See, if you put it on, even without a bolt, that hold it. That lamp, you see, shouldn't-- see, the bracket was like that. In here was a hole, in here was a  hole. I could make 40 a day, 50 a day. I only made six or seven a day. Six?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest the time, I took it for myself. So what-- I took that powder, and I-- and I was supposed to braze it with-- weld it with brass. That, you call it brazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we-- so I welded with the steel rods. And I used the-- the welding-- the welding-- the brass for-- for chips. I took that-- the brass and cut it that way, like chips-- sharp here and sharp here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, how you know it's going to work? So we tried it out. We had to try out of the-- maybe it's not powder-- it's no good. So I had some fellow from my city, Zalman Lewenthal. He worked in the crematorium. And I had another few fellows . And we tried it out in the little-- in the little boxes, with matches. Was there a large noise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Powder? Sure. Sure. It has to be a large noise. So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you do it, without anyone hearing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what I say. So I made a-- we-- we-- with matches, we made powder. We-- I want to know if it's blowing. That's all. So we put a little bit powder in them little boxes. But that smell is very bad, when you-- when you blow it on. And the SS is over there. So what-- with big ventilation, we all-- we-- we let the whole ventilation in crematorium, and now we blow it. We said, if that blows, that's OK. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made this. But that's not enough to blow a-- a big building. So I took an oxygen bottle-- oxygen bottle, you know? You know what-- you what oxygen in? Acetylene? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't-- if you want to cut something, steel, what you cut it. You need the oxygen in acetylene. So you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took two bottles like that, because I got to go to work at the crematorium. And I took them to the crematorium over there. And I hooked up the hoses, everything. I could have take out everything what you want. I had three-- another bottle, an extra two, made special. I told you what I made with my own bottle. So what's happened? I left this in the crematorium. And over there, in the-- at the oven-- Say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the oven. You know what the oven was. There was a fellow from my city too. His name was Worona. Worona means a black beard. In the old-- in the old country, he was a-- a glazer. And to make windows. You know, you build a house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You go from-- from the house there-- they build new houses, so you have to have windows. He was-- so we-- I talked to him. And we left the bottle over there, say. Then, I need another bottle. I went back to Auschwitz. And I said, I need another bottle. Where is the-- when I give, I have to give the-- the empty bag. What if I brought bread and salami back? I could have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING] --do anything. So how I going to bring it back? So I took a bottle. With that bottle, I couldn't for a-- don't make [INAUDIBLE] in the world. I could bring-- I had to bring it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you've made the grenades--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide them? How could--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'll tell you where. Wait a minute. There was a boy. He's in Mexico. He's alive. He was there. He was a peeper. You know? He looked after the-- over kapo, Finderkamp-- Finderkamp, and then the-- then the lager commandant--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hefling. So he was over there with the-- he was a [INAUDIBLE] short-- his name  is Galek-- Berele Galek. So I-- well, which place could it be better to hide? Like you hide, like, in-- in the kapo's place, the German. Who-- who's going to look over there?

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I gave it to him to hide. So he told me where he hide it, next to the swimming pool.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] This was not that good. The important thing were the bottles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bottles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- and a few guns. I had a few guns. The guns, I got at-- you know, in Stalingrad, from the airplanes. You know, they used to-- they brought-- they brought the airplanes back to Auschwitz with-- with-- for to dismantle them. So 90% of the Russians worked over there. You know, but the biggest problem with them, we couldn't trust them. They drinked-- they drinked that-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know what you call that-- that-- that-- gas for the airplane. It was so strong, they got blind. Some got blind. Well anyway, we bought some from them.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the Russians eat flesh, human flesh? There was nothing to eat, so they took out the living to eat-- the Russians. Mr. Silber-- OK, now, how did you get-- how did you hide these from the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's nothing to hide. I need the bottles to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put on two gauges with the hoses. I put that-- actually, four gauges. And I had four bottles. I tell you, I could have take out a whole car.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could have take out, at least 30, 40 salamies from the slaughterhouse. I used to go to the slaughterhouse-- anything what I wanted, I could have taken one.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I done? See, this is a bottle-- oxygen. Here, in Canada, the bottles are round. If you stay with a torch, they can [INAUDIBLE]. You know what I mean? In Europe, the bottles are like this. They've got the-- a little thing out here, like it's swaying. [INAUDIBLE]. So what happened? I took that off, took that out, and took it at the-- on the lathe. You know a lathe? You know about a lathe? You were-- you-- you make the steel smaller or bigger. I took it out, and make it exactly a little-- a little smaller. So we cut it on the lathe. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this, this fits exactly here. So when I want to take it out from the slaughterhouse, I just took that out and filled it up inside. We-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And OK-- now, how did you-- now-- OK, so-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people-- I know that you-- you-- you mentioned that nobody knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you have a group together to-- tell me how that happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what-- what do you call a group? Everybody [INAUDIBLE] get a-- we get a-- had only one-- one thing, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget, and don't forgive. You haven't got nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like I thought the head was a Jewish fellow. He was from Suwalki. His name was Kaminski, the head over kapo. So I thought he's against us. Well then, what happened? He was with us. Oh, but he didn't want to show up. So what's happened? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Took him out for-- for a walk. He took a walk around the crematorium. He took out a gun and killed him. Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we know right away what's going to happen. But they know it already, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how many-- OK, so tell me the day, the night, that you-- you went to blow up the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that Jukel Worona.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Windsor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Jukel Worona, who worked in the crematorium, that went and worked on the block. He keep for me the guns with the-- with the-- with the soot, with the-- with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, continue. So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after, when we blow that up, we were-- everybody's scared what's going to happen. So they want to take this under commander come all night-- one day-- night with this under commander. You can't sleep anymore. You got to be isolated. So they made him-- there was over there right away, beds. And then, this under commander in the crematorium to sleep for there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went over there. They can't come more in the camp. To come in camp-- there's a few only, and that's it. Well, with the few, Kaminski come. So we didn't know if he's with us, or against us. So what's happened later when they killed him. So the whole thing was mixed up again. So one day, I come on the-- on the-- on the block. I heard some-- they-- they're looking for a transport. So what? Do you want to take this under commander for a transport? This under commander for a transport takes, maybe, four or five days. You got on a run, a run, a run. They give you three loaf of bread, two-- two salamis.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, you don't want to eat it, because you want to keep it for the next day. The next day, they give you an old shirt. Well, this under commander know it. You're going to go out four or five days. You're going to come in, maybe, who knows, [INAUDIBLE], SS, you can take him and kill him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they-- they said didn't-- they're not going. When they said they're not going, SS guy come in there. They're not going to go. So that Jukel took them bottles and shoved it in the oven. And that was blowed. And after-- after--

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, when I come back-- I told the girls a couple days before, they should be ready-- get dressed, wear their shoes. You know, you're in wintertime-- underwear. [LAUGHS] My wife, and that girl-- a few girls were, still alive. I did-- I was just-- I never saw a girl in 40-odd years. I just was in-- in-- what you call? In-- in Manhattan. She was over there too. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, you asked me how I took out the Jewish girls by the [INAUDIBLE], how-- how I took her-- I had a place. She did-- she-- this-- we built the barracks. That was a-- that was a barrack. No, no, it wasn't. That-- yes, that was a barrack. So you had to dig a little. No foundation, or nothing. So you had to dig a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they all did. We put it here. So when we went in, the bottle was empty. We always made the room for more Jews to come. We thought the whole world that Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] are going to come. From Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from all the-- so this-- how far was he? Where was Rommel? Montgomery? See? You know? Rommel got killed by Hitler, not by somebody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here, there was a big mountain from dirt. So we-- I took-- we took some food over there and support that, that the wood-- that the dirt shouldn't collapse. And we went in from here, took up a few-- a few boards, and we let the men in over there. After three days, you couldn't go out. You couldn't move. So how you eat? So we give them a few bread. Salami, never, because the dogs smell it. You know, live things-- they smell the salami. So he was over there. For a few days, the siren. It's quiet. Three days, you can't go out. So we-- we took out that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Molly, we took her out different. Molly, she was like a [NON-ENGLISH] She was a [NON-ENGLISH] And like, she worked in the-- where you-- people coming in, and people were-- if you need anything, you go over there to-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], now, a place where-- where they marking down all the dead, all the alives. She worked over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she would run around from one place and another. And she had that Edek Galinski, like a boyfriend. So Edek, how can I get him out? So I took that-- that soot, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I gaved him that a day before. And he took that soot and hide it. It was over the [NON-ENGLISH] bunker, you know, where they keep the potatoes for winter, a bunker. So how can he see Molly? And he put himself out like a soldier, like an SS man. So how can he see Molly? And how can Molly see him? He-- he's a soldier. There's lots of soldiers where she can go into the wrong soldier. You know what I mean? So we were talking. I didn't know myself. I didn't know too much. The Russian taught me a little bit. So what happened? He told-- I told him, here is the soot. And here, there is a roll of paper. Here is a roll of paper. That Molly, when they see a soldier with a roll of paper, she going to walk through, because she had to go through Gelsen ghetto. You had to go through the-- the Bergen, where the soldiers stay. And you are the-- you see-- you see a person with a ball from the toilet, with a-- with a-- what do you call a one-piece suit? A blue suit? A worker's suit? What do you call that? A uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: A uni--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only has zipper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, one-- the one-piece?

speaker: interviewee
text: One piece. What do you call that? What do you call that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Overalls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Overalls. That's right. [LAUGHS] Overall. You, Molly-- see, a woman is different. The back is different, entirely. You know what I mean? You give a-- you give it in a dress, no good. A little high in a woman. A difference in the back, like a-- like a man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she-- she go-- and the overall is loose. And she took-- she took that ball on in her head, because you have to see the hair like that. She took-- and Edek saw-- and Edek saw-- and she saw Edek with a roll  of paper. She had to walk to each other. It had to be exactly 12 o'clock, because they were hungry too-- the soldiers who stay on the first thing on the-- on the morning. So as soon as he come over there, he showed him-- he showed him the-- the piece of paper. He didn't want to even look. He was hungry. He let him go. And he went away. For two weeks they didn't catch him. After two weeks, they catched him. And they hanged him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, she-- she cut herself with a razor blade-- the veins. And him, they hanged. He didn't say nothing. I was staying like that, to look on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, what I going to do? So I come, like, back and forth on the block. So the head man-- I wrote a book, So he told me, Godel, there's a good transport. You want to go away? Go. I said, listen, how can I go away? I got a brother here. I got a friend. We were four-- we were four fellows keeping together.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK, don't worry. I'll fix it up for you. So OK, he fixed it up for me. We went on a transport. As soon as I come on the train, people recognized me. They put me on, maybe, 20, 30 loaf of bread, maybe 20 salamis. Anything I wanted, [LAUGHS] they gave me, bribed me right away-- good shoes. And they brought me a sweater, a good coat, a few shirts-- everything. And then, we went on the transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back onto a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the date you arrived, or the day you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stop? On-- on the way, did  you stop in-- in any towns, or anywhere along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We stopped after, when we went on the-- on the dead march.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll come to that. Tell me about your stay in Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sachsenhausen? [LAUGHS] Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, over there, I was lucky too. I-- I-- they find out that some people come from Auschwitz. The people come, tell them what's happened maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sachsenhausen was a big camp too. He was over there, Leon Bloom. Did you hear from Leon Bloom? Yeah. He was over there. And over there in Sachsenhausen, it could make America-- [? Vienna ?] broke, because they made money-- false money. They made-- what you call it? Pounds. The pounds looked like cigarette paper. You ever had an English pound? Like cigarette paper. So they made them. They were false. But they-- they were-- they were so good, nobody could-- how I know it? Because I had two boys from mine city, only still alive in New York. They were named Biermann. They were watchmakers. So they made it. In the meantime, when I come to Sachsenhausen, I finally found out that our transport from Auschwitz, from [INAUDIBLE] over there. Oh where-- so he comes over there, and he calls it-- when you come? When you come? Anybody from Ciechanow? I say, yes. [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] That was that neighbor. We were brought up together. And not Semetic bigots. Couldn't be a bigot in the camp we was going. So in the meantime, when I came over there, they wanted to take away my shoes. They wanted-- and my clothes. You know, but they were so good. So he stayed with me. And he went over there. He was an old fellow, [INAUDIBLE]. He said, listen, don't you touch that [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and from over there, it was the few days. And I said, oh, the whole [NON-ENGLISH], right? The whole-- I mean, factory, where I used to work. The-- the Polacks, they brought him. They brought him to Sachsenhausen too, like to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] factory. I don't know that it was over there. But they make airplanes-- Messerschmitt. I don't if you ever hear. Say it again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Messerschmitt. And I was a few days over there. Then, they took us to Kaufering. Then-- that was before, on-- before Christmas-- on Christmas time-- took us to Kaufering and Lage Rhine. I went to [INAUDIBLE] over there. From over there, they took us to-- to Lage Leven. I was with a girl tehre. She-- she-- she don't remember. You know, it used to be Raditsky family. She didn't-- [INAUDIBLE]. They still live on that street in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- so you were in-- you were in Landsberg-- Landsberg-Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg-Kaufering-- from December, end of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of March. What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I didn't-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your routine like there? Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You woke up, and there was a roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing over there. I went over there and talked to the Jews. Listen, I think it's the end. You don't have to give him away your loaf of bread. Give him a smile. That's enough. If you give him  a smile, you won't-- [LAUGHS] you'll hold the people up. Don't go over there. [INAUDIBLE]. In the meantime, some Hungarian Jews come, some doctors. In that time, I met Mengele, who were in the [INAUDIBLE], in the same camp. And Mengele met the head doctor from the Jews over there. Like, they went together in Vienna, in school-- and Dr. Freibaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were talking every day, Mengele with the doctor. Then a few weeks before, they took us out for the [INAUDIBLE]. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the [INAUDIBLE], we walked out slowly, slowly, because we let go the ladies ahead. And if you-- the men go ahead, before, the ladies wouldn't catch up. And anybody wouldn't catch up, they shoot them. They killed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went. I organized. I went to the ladies, and I told them, listen, go slowly. We made [INAUDIBLE], we made maybe 15, 20 kilometers. And we'd stay for an hour. We were lucky if it didn't rain. But what good was it? With the rain, you couldn't get up after. We got stiff. You know what I mean? What were you wearing? And it-- it was-- what-- well, first of all, what was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what could-- could happen [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like in Oct-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: In March.

speaker: interviewer
text: In March.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it warm, or cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold. Cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what were you wearing on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, on my feet? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Just the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My shoes that I got. I told you, good shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people went on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the march? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, oh, on the march was lots of people. I mean, in mine column-- in my-- it was columns. I was here. Two miles farther was a-- another march. Two far-- two miles farther, another march. It was lots of them. They liquidated all the camps. In my column, was a few hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few hundred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not two-- a few hundred.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they feed you at all on the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- absolute nothing. That is how we survived. We had-- we had, maybe, food for a week. So we men-- we-- the men refused to eat. We should cook the-- for the ladies a little bit hot water in that-- that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how we survived? So it was, when we marched, there was-- [TEARFUL] there was lots-- lots of [INAUDIBLE] civilian-- Hollandish, Czechs, French. So [INAUDIBLE], they all stand the road in the city. That's how we were. So he want to risk his life. And then [INAUDIBLE] he put me in [TEARFUL] a piece of bread, two apples, sometimes. And how long did you walk every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. We figured out [INAUDIBLE] we stopped. And we made no more like 15, 20 kilometers a day. No more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they allowed you to rest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't go further. They were-- [INAUDIBLE] they were tired too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened when you stopped-- when you stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] we stopped. So we couldn't go further. And all of a sudden, the Wermacht come-- the general from Wermacht come. And he starts to talk to-- to that commandant from the SS. Where is he taking us? The war is over. He shouldn't take us farther. He said, OK. The meantime, he took us, because commandant-- the general from the SS, he said, no, you can't leave them. You have to take them over to the next bush, and maybe we kill them. But that-- that-- that was the talking. [TEARFUL] But that Wermacht knew what was going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? He come back, and he started to scream. Listen, you want that I should kill you? After all, I am the general. You better take them to the next little village and let them stay over there. And that's where we got liberated by the Americans-- come in-- [TEARFUL] Can you remember that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. American Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked us a few words. What's happening? How many people got killed? So we told him. We said, a few hundred, but there was no Jews. They took [INAUDIBLE] that commandant he shouldn't take us. He should give the-- wait another-- he shouldn't be scared for the Jews. The Jews know we're never going to revolt [INAUDIBLE]. And the American Army [? lost a friend. ?] Jewish. And we're going to help him. It happened like that, he didn't-- so anyway, I didn't trust him then. I didn't trust him. I didn't-- I didn't trust him. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Silber, I think we'll take a little break.
